text: First, examine the wound and identify the point of bleeding.
Then, pack combat gauze directly over the source of bleeding and ensure you have good initial contact.
Continue to pack the entire dressing while maintaining good contact.
Use of the entire dressing will ensure the proper amount of agent will be delivered into the wound.
Lastly, apply two to three minutes of direct pressure.
This period of direct pressure is important to ensure the hemostatic agent is properly effective in the wound.
Periodically, check placement of the dressing and ensure it is still properly placed and there is no active bleeding from the edges.
Do not lift the dressing to reassess.
Ensure proper positioning and continue to hold pressure and let the active ingredient work.
Occasionally with difficult placements, the bandage will become completely soaked through and there will still be active bleeding.
In these situations, you must entirely remove the old gauze and replace it with a completely new roll using the same steps as previously mentioned.
After two to three minutes of direct pressure, bandage the wound to secure the dressing in place.
This can be done with any common field dressing or elastic bandage.
In these cases, it is helpful to wipe away any pooled blood.
Do this with your hand or a wad of cotton gauze.
This helps to identify the wound and ensure direct contact with the point of bleeding.
This is the only additional step required.
All other steps are the same as a typical application.
Identify the wound.
Pack over the point of bleeding.
Pack the entire dressing.
Apply direct pressure for two to three minutes.
